title: A third place: a separate file
checkLabels.t7: You didn't copy the styles over
checkLabels.t6: The link is in the head
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of adding one line. Bring the code back.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was. The `styles.css` file is sitting there ready, but the page doesn't know about it.
hint2.untouched: Look at the two tabs above the editor. One is the page, the other is the stylesheet. The line that connects them is missing.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: `<link>` closes itself. There is no `</link>` — if you wrote one, remove it.
why.no-h1: There is no heading. You deleted the `<h1>` instead of adding the link.
hint2.no-h1: You're adding one line to the `<head>`. The body stays untouched.
why.no-p: There is not a single paragraph left. You deleted them.
hint2.no-p: The two lines of text are fine. Only the link to the file is missing.
why.not-linked: The page can't find `styles.css`. Either there is no `<link>`, or the path in it points at a file that doesn't exist.
hint2.not-linked: Look at the name on the second tab above the editor. Letter by letter. Then compare it with what you wrote in `href`.
hint3.not-linked: `styles.css` and `style.css` are different files. One missing character and the browser looks for something that isn't there. It doesn't complain. It just doesn't colour anything.
hint4.not-linked: Add the line inside `<head>`. In `href` write the exact file name — `styles.css`.
why.link-in-body: The link is outside `<head>`. It works, but that isn't where it belongs — same as `<style>` in lesson 02.
hint2.link-in-body: The browser needs to know the styles BEFORE it paints the text. That's why they go in the head.
hint3.link-in-body: Put them at the end and the page is already painted when the styles arrive — so the browser recomputes the layout and paints again. On a slow connection people see the flash.
why.h1-no-color: The heading isn't dark blue. The link found the file, but the heading is still black.
hint2.h1-no-color: Open the `styles.css` tab. The `h1` rule is there and it's correct. So the problem isn't in it.
hint3.h1-no-color: Have you edited the stylesheet? It's given to you finished — this lesson is about the link, not about writing CSS.
hint4.h1-no-color: Restore both files as they were and add only the `<link>` line.
why.p-no-color: The paragraphs aren't brown. The `p` rule does exist in the file.
hint2.p-no-color: The check looks at both paragraphs, not the first one.
why.wrote-style-tag: You wrote a `<style>` block in the page. It works — but that's exactly what we're avoiding here.
hint2.wrote-style-tag: The styles are already written, in the other file. Don't copy them — link them.
hint3.wrote-style-tag: A copy is a duplicate. Tomorrow the owner wants a different colour, you change it in the file, and the page keeps using the old copy. That's the very problem `<link>` solves.
hint4.wrote-style-tag: Remove the `<style>` block. Leave only the `<link>` line.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. You're adding a link, not deleting text.
blocks.1.text: Look above the editor. There are two tabs now. `index.html` is the page. `styles.css` is new — inside it are the styles you fixed in lesson 03, moved out.
blocks.2.text: Open it. You'll see rules and nothing else — not one HTML tag, no `<style>`. You don't write those in a CSS file. The file IS the stylesheet.
blocks.4.text: Right now the page has no idea that file exists. That's why it's black. One line in its head says so:
blocks.6.items.0: `rel` means *relationship* — how the linked file relates to the page. For styles, always `stylesheet`. The same tag with `rel="icon"` attaches the tab icon.
blocks.6.items.1: `href` is the path to the file. Same paths as with links and images.
blocks.6.items.2: The tag closes itself. There is no `</link>`.
blocks.6.items.3: `type="text/css"` isn't needed — that's the default value. You'll see it in old code. Don't copy it.
blocks.8.text: The cafe opens a second page: the menu. A third: contact. Each writes the same one line and gets the same styles. Change the colour in the file and it changes on all three. With `<style>` you'd be copying it three times and remembering all three on every change.
blocks.9.text: And something you can't see: the browser keeps the file once it has downloaded it. The second page doesn't fetch it again. A site carrying its styles inside sends them all over again with every page.
blocks.10.text: `<link>` is the normal way. `<style>` — for a single page or while you're experimenting. The attribute on the tag — almost never.
blocks.12.text: Add the line inside `<head>` of `index.html`. Don't touch `styles.css` — it's finished. The result is the same as the end of lesson 03.
blocks.15.items.0: `<link rel="stylesheet" href="...">` in `<head>` connects the page to an external file.
blocks.15.items.1: A CSS file has no tags in it. Only rules.
blocks.15.items.2: One file, many pages. You change it in one place.
blocks.15.items.3: A wrong file name gives you no error. It gives you a page with no styles.
blocks.16.text: From here on `index.html` is given and stays untouched. You write in `styles.css`. Three places is all there is — the remaining ninety-four lessons are about WHAT goes inside. One thing to keep in mind: a page can carry several of these lines, and they apply in the order they appear. Who wins when they disagree — lesson 53.
walkthrough.0.text: One line. `rel="stylesheet"` says what kind of relationship this is — there are others, `rel="icon"` for the tab icon for instance. Without `rel` the browser doesn't know it's a stylesheet and won't load it.
walkthrough.1.text: `href="styles.css"` is a path with no folder, so the file sits next to the page. Get one letter wrong and there's no message — nothing just gets coloured. This is the most common reason a beginner decides their CSS is broken when it was never even read.
walkthrough.2.text: The line is in `<head>`. It works in `<body>` too, but the browser has already painted the text when the styles arrive, and has to recompute the layout.
walkthrough.3.text: I didn't touch `styles.css`. The temptation is to move something into it while you're in there — but the lesson checks exactly that. A copy of the styles inside the page works today and drifts from the file tomorrow.
